answer.answer:
Algolia ranks search results by evaluating a sequence of **ranking criteria** in order, using a **tie-breaking algorithm**: it compares records on the first criterion, and only if there’s a tie does it move to the next criterion, and so on until the tie is broken. If nothing differentiates two records, Algolia can ultimately fall back to **objectID** ordering. ([tie-breaking explanation](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean))

## Algolia’s ranking criteria (default order)
In the Dashboard’s Ranking Criteria panel, you’ll typically see criteria like the following, shown in the same order as your **Ranking and Sorting** configuration:

- **Number of typos**: how many typos were needed to match the query  
- **Geolocation**: distance from the search location (when geo is used)  
- **Matching words**: number of query words matched  
- **Matching filters**: number of filters matched  
- **Proximity distance**: how close matched words are to each other  
- **Best matching attribute**: which attribute matched, based on attribute priority  
- **Position**: where the matched words appear in the attribute  
- **Exact word count**: number of exact (no-typo) matches  
- **Custom ranking**: your business metrics (for example popularity, ratings)  

([ranking criteria breakdown + dashboard panel](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard))

## How tie-breaking works (step-by-step)
Algolia’s **tie-breaking algorithm** ranks records sequentially across criteria:

1. Sort all matching records by the **first** criterion.
2. For records that are tied, sort those tied records by the **second** criterion.
3. Continue criterion-by-criterion until the tie is broken.
4. If there are still ties after the default criteria, Algolia uses **Custom Ranking** attributes to try to break the tie.
5. If there’s *still* no difference, Algolia ranks based on **objectID** (you may see a message like “ranked based on objectID”). ([objectID fallback details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean), [custom ranking used to break ties](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used))

## Notes on geo and ties
For **geo ranking**, Algolia groups distances into buckets based on `aroundPrecision`. Hits within the same bucket are considered equal for the geo criterion, letting subsequent textual criteria break ties. If `aroundPrecision` is too small, geo can effectively dominate the ordering. ([geo sorting and aroundPrecision](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406216902929-Why-is-my-search-being-sorted-by-geo-and-not-taking-into-account-other-ranking-criteria))

## Why ranking changes might not “apply”
Some features (like Rules “boost and bury” via `optionalFilters`) are evaluated at the **filters** ranking criterion, so they only influence ordering when earlier criteria don’t already break the tie. If you put a strong sort/custom ranking above it, it may prevent the boost/bury from taking effect. ([boost/bury and filters criterion](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9821011449233-Why-is-the-boost-and-bury-feature-not-applied))

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

Why is the 'boost and bury' feature not applied?
The boost and bury features from Rules are based on the optionalFilters parameter. The are two main reasons you may not see items being boosted or buried when you apply a Rule to do so.
These apply to both Rules and Recommend Rules.
1. Ranking and Sorting Configuration
Optional filters are evaluated at the "filters" ranking criterion within the default ranking criteria. 
Because Algolia uses the tie-breaking algorithm, the filters criterion affects the ranking only when precedence criteria do not break the tie.
So for example, if there is a sort-by attribute that is ranked higher than the filters ranking criterion in the Ranking and sorting configuration, boost and bury features will not take effect because the sort by attribute has precedence over any other ranking criterion:

 
In this case it is advised to only add a sort-by attribute to replica indices. Otherwise, lowering the custom ranking criteria below the standard default ranking criteria would enable the proper functioning of boost and bury features.
 
2. Declaring your attribute for faceting
To use an attribute in a Rule that boosts or buries items, you must first declare it as an attributesForFaceting. You can do this either via the:

API: https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/faceting/how-to/declaring-attributes-for-faceting/

Dashboard: https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/faceting/how-to/declaring-attributes-for-faceting-with-dashboard/
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9821011449233-Why-is-the-boost-and-bury-feature-not-applied

sources.text:
Why is my search being sorted by geo and not taking into account other ranking criteria?
When ranking hits, geo distances are grouped into ranges of aroundPrecision size. All hits within the same range are considered equal with respect to the geo ranking parameter. For example, if you set aroundPrecision to 100, any two objects lying in the range [0, 99] from the searched location will be considered equal; same for [100, 199], [200, 299], etc. This will allow items that are relatively close to be ranked the same in the "geo" criterion and let the textual criteria do the tie breaking.
In your current setting, aroundPrecision could be too small a number meaning that unless you move geo to the bottom, everything is effectively getting sorted by how far away it is on a meter by meter basis.  In general, we do not advise changing the default ranking formula so it would be preferable to change the aroundPrecision.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406216902929-Why-is-my-search-being-sorted-by-geo-and-not-taking-into-account-other-ranking-criteria

sources.text:
How can I troubleshoot relevancy using Algolia dashboard?
When debugging relevancy issues, the Algolia dashboard provides several visual tools to help you understand why records are ranked the way they are for a given query. This guide walks through how to use the Ranking Criteria panel, and explains each ranking criterion you may encounter.Using the Ranking Criteria panelIn the Browse tab on the Algolia dashboard, each record in the results list includes a small medal icon at the bottom-right corner.When you hover over this icon, a panel appears showing the ranking criteria and their values for that record.This panel explains exactly why a record is ranked in its current position relative to others, by displaying how it performed for each ranking criterion:The criteria are shown in the same order as your Ranking and Sorting configuration, which is important: Algolia ranks results using a tie-breaking algorithm that evaluates records sequentially across ranking criteria, stopping as soon as one criterion produces a difference between records. You can change the order of the default criteria, but you shouldn’t. The out-of-the-box ranking order is best for the majority of use cases. Below is a breakdown of what each Ranking criterion means and how to interpret its value in the ranking panel:
Number of typos: Typos required to match the query
Geolocation: Distance from the search location
Matching words: Number of query words matched
Matching filters: Number of filters matched
Proximity distance: Distance between matched words
Best matching attribute: Attribute matched, by priority
Position: Position of matched words in the attribute
Exact word count: Number of exact (no-typo) matches
Custom ranking: Custom ranking value defined by your business metrics (e.g., popularity, ratings)
The ranking criteria panel helps you clearly understand why a record ranks where it does and pinpoint the source of any relevancy issues.Query Alternatives viewCombined with the Ranking Criteria panel, using the Query Alternatives view, gives you a more complete picture of how Algolia interprets queries and ranks results. If you toggle Query Alternatives on the dashboard, you can see how Algolia interprets the query you typed:For a detailed walkthrough, see How to use the Query Alternatives view in the Algolia Dashboard.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard

sources.text:
What does "This result is ranked based on objectID" mean?
The "This result is ranked based on objectID" message indicates that none of your ranking criteria differentiates those results in terms of relevancy.
Algolia uses a tie-breaking algorithm for ranking search results. By default, it works as follows (as described in this document):

It orders all matching records according to the first criterion (number of typos, so exact matches rank first).
For all tied records, it orders them according to the second criterion (geolocation - if applicable).
If there are still tied records (with the same geolocation), it orders them according to the third criterion (filters) and so on, until each record in the search results has a distinct ranking position.
If, after going through the first seven criteria, there are still tied records, Algolia uses each of your custom ranking attributes to break the tie.

After going through all steps and if the custom ranking does not break the tie, Algolia ranks the items based on their objectID, which is the case that this message indicates.
 
If you'd like to avoid such situation and make your ranking criteria meaningful, we recommend reviewing your ranking strategy and consider adding or modifying custom rankings that can break the tie of such items.
You can also find the detail of the default ranking criterion in this document.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean

sources.text:
Why is my custom ranking attribute not being used?
Algolia finds the best matches and ranks the search results with its default ranking strategy, using a tie-breaking algorithm. First, it orders ALL matching records using the first criterion (typo by default). For all tie matching records it orders them using the second criterion (geo by default) and if there are still tied matching records, then it orders them according to the third criterion (filters by default), and so on. After going through 8 ranking criteria, it uses any Custom Ranking to break the tie - the engine will go on until there’s no more ties to break.

You can hover your mouse over the little badge shaped icon in the bottom right of each record to check which tie breaker ranked the record in that specific position.
You can increase the importance of your custom ranking criteria by expanding the default ranking criteria and moving your custom criteria up the order. Be extremely careful when doing this - the default order is considered optimal in the majority of cases.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used

answer.answer:
Algolia ranks results by evaluating a sequence of **ranking criteria in order**, using a **tie‑breaking algorithm**: it applies the first criterion to all matching records, then only uses the next criterion to re-order records that are still tied, and so on until the tie is broken (or it falls back to `objectID`). See the step-by-step description in: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean

## Ranking criteria (default relevance criteria)
These are the ranking criteria you’ll commonly see (and how they’re described in the Dashboard’s Ranking Criteria panel): https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard

- **Number of typos**: typos required to match the query  
- **Geolocation**: distance from the search location  
- **Matching words**: number of query words matched  
- **Matching filters**: number of filters matched  
- **Proximity distance**: distance between matched words  
- **Best matching attribute**: attribute matched, by priority  
- **Position**: position of matched words in the attribute  
- **Exact word count**: number of exact (no-typo) matches  
- **Custom ranking**: custom ranking value defined by your business metrics (e.g., popularity, ratings)

A support overview also lists the relevance criteria as: **Typo, Geo, Words, Filters, Proximity, Attribute, Exact, Custom**, plus **attribute and proximity combinations**: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/38449520711057-Why-are-the-search-results-in-Algolia-different-from-other-search-engines-extensions-and-integrations

## How tie-breaking works (what happens when records are “equal”)
Algolia’s tie-breaking is sequential across criteria (and stops as soon as one criterion differentiates records): https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard

A concrete description of the default tie-break sequence is here: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean
- Order all matching records by the **first** criterion (e.g., **number of typos**).
- For records still tied, order those by the **second** criterion (e.g., **geolocation**, if applicable).
- Continue criterion-by-criterion (filters, etc.) until records become distinct.
- If ties remain after the “standard” criteria, Algolia uses your **custom ranking attributes** to break the tie.
- If it *still* can’t break the tie, Algolia ranks tied items by **`objectID`** (that’s what the “ranked based on objectID” message means).

## A couple of practical tie-break implications (examples from support)
- **Geo tie ranges:** with geosearch, distances are grouped into ranges of `aroundPrecision`. Hits within the same range are considered equal for the `geo` criterion, which “lets the textual criteria do the tie breaking”: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406216902929-Why-is-my-search-being-sorted-by-geo-and-not-taking-into-account-other-ranking-criteria
- **Rules “boost/bury” via `optionalFilters`:** `optionalFilters` are evaluated at the **filters** ranking criterion, and “because Algolia uses the tie-breaking algorithm, the filters criterion affects the ranking only when precedence criteria do not break the tie.” If you have a sort-by/custom criterion above `filters`, it can prevent boost/bury from taking effect: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9821011449233-Why-is-the-boost-and-bury-feature-not-applied

What’s your use case for tie-breaking—are you trying to understand a specific “ranked based on objectID” situation, or are you designing a custom ranking to ensure ties get broken earlier?

sources.text:
Why are the search results in Algolia different from other search engines, extensions, and integrations?
If you're switching to Algolia after previously using a search engine that's built-in to your website or integrated into your website by using a third-party extension, then you might wonder why the ordering of your results may be different after adding your records and configuring the index. There are a few reasons why the order of the search results may be different.We use a different relevance criteriaTo order the search results, we use a ranking criteria to define relevance. For our algorithm, the search results rank based on the following criteria:
Typo
Geo
Words
Filters
Proximity
Attribute
Exact
Custom
Attribute and proximity combinations
You can find out more about what this criteria means in our documentation: Ranking Critiera. Searchable Attributes may not include every attribute you'd likeThe Searchable Attributes list is for adding attributes you'd like the search engine to search through while retrieving results. Please check that list to ensure that all preferred attributes are included. Rules, Dynamic Re-Ranking, and Personalization could be appliedBeyond applying the relevance criteria while ranking results, other factors could be at play due to an index's configuration. If you're using Rules, Dynamic Re-Ranking, or Personalization, then your search results' ranking could be affected. You can find out more about how these features are applied to ranking here: Effect on Relevance. While we can't comment on how other search engines and extensions rank their results, we hope this article gives you information regarding how Algolia ranks your search results.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/38449520711057-Why-are-the-search-results-in-Algolia-different-from-other-search-engines-extensions-and-integrations

sources.text:
How can I add custom ranking attributes using the API Client?
This article will walk you through the steps required to add custom ranking attributes to your application using an API client.
1. First, specify the custom ranking attributes using the setSettings method. For example,
index.setSettings({  customRanking:[    'desc(popularity)',    'asc(price)'  ]}).then(()=>{  // done});
You can see code examples here. 2. Then, pass the custom criterion at the bottom of the ranking array.
ranking: [
  // the `asc` and `desc` modifiers must be placed at the top // if you are configuring an index for sorting purposes only
  'asc(attribute1)',
  'desc(attribute2)',
  'typo',
  'geo',
  'words',
  'filters',
  'proximity',
  'attribute',
  'exact',
  'custom'
]

sources.text:
Why is my custom ranking attribute not being used?
Algolia finds the best matches and ranks the search results with its default ranking strategy, using a tie-breaking algorithm. First, it orders ALL matching records using the first criterion (typo by default). For all tie matching records it orders them using the second criterion (geo by default) and if there are still tied matching records, then it orders them according to the third criterion (filters by default), and so on. After going through 8 ranking criteria, it uses any Custom Ranking to break the tie - the engine will go on until there’s no more ties to break.

You can hover your mouse over the little badge shaped icon in the bottom right of each record to check which tie breaker ranked the record in that specific position.
You can increase the importance of your custom ranking criteria by expanding the default ranking criteria and moving your custom criteria up the order. Be extremely careful when doing this - the default order is considered optimal in the majority of cases.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used

sources.text:
What does "This result is ranked based on objectID" mean?
The "This result is ranked based on objectID" message indicates that none of your ranking criteria differentiates those results in terms of relevancy.
Algolia uses a tie-breaking algorithm for ranking search results. By default, it works as follows (as described in this document):

It orders all matching records according to the first criterion (number of typos, so exact matches rank first).
For all tied records, it orders them according to the second criterion (geolocation - if applicable).
If there are still tied records (with the same geolocation), it orders them according to the third criterion (filters) and so on, until each record in the search results has a distinct ranking position.
If, after going through the first seven criteria, there are still tied records, Algolia uses each of your custom ranking attributes to break the tie.

After going through all steps and if the custom ranking does not break the tie, Algolia ranks the items based on their objectID, which is the case that this message indicates.
 
If you'd like to avoid such situation and make your ranking criteria meaningful, we recommend reviewing your ranking strategy and consider adding or modifying custom rankings that can break the tie of such items.
You can also find the detail of the default ranking criterion in this document.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean

sources.text:
How can I promote more than 300 products without pinning them?
Currently, there is a hard limit of 300 items that can be pinned using Rules. This limit ensures optimal performance levels for both your search results and the Visual Editor. However, you may find on occasions that you want to promote more records than this. Although this isn’t recommended from a UX perspective, in such cases it’s often more efficient to use optionalFilters or ranking logic instead of using a rule to manually pin each product.Option 1: Using optionalFiltersInstead of pinning each product individually, you can add a boolean attribute to your records and use optionalFilters to boost records that match a specific criteria. For example:{
  "optionalFilters":"in_stock:true"
}This approach works well when you want particular products to appear first but are not concerned with fixing their exact positions.You can read more on promoting records with optionalFilters here.Option 2: Use a “Pinned Position” attributeThere may be a more complex scenario where you want your products to appear in specific positions.If you can determine these positions, you can add them to your records as an attribute, such as: "pinned_position":1. Products without a pinned position can be assigned a single default value.ExampleNote this example only works if the same pinning logic applies across all queries as it will be applied at an index level.Imagine you have 2,000 records, and you want 500 of them to appear in fixed positions.
Add an attribute like pinned_position to your records.
For items that must appear in a specific order, assign a unique numeric value (1, 2, 3, etc.).
For all other items, assign a single very high value (eg. 9999).
You can then:
Use a sort-by attribute for exhaustive sorting if the order must be exact.
Add pinned_position to your custom ranking if you still want Algolia’s relevance criteria to influence the order.
Please note this is an implementation example, and may be completely different for your use case. The Algolia Support team cannot give tailored implementation advice beyond the documented functionality of our products.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8673737386769-How-can-I-promote-more-than-300-products-without-pinning-them

sources.text:
Why is my multi-word synonym not matching as expected?

Multi-word synonyms behave differently from single-word synonyms.
Matching only starts after the full phrase begins to match
For multi-word synonyms, the engine only matches the synonym when the last word starts to match. For example, with a one-way synonym:
New York > NY

Searching for ne won’t trigger the synonym
Searching for new yo will start triggering it

Multi-word synonyms match by sequence expression, meaning the engine considers multi-word synonyms as a single phrase and they must be in the correct order with nothing in-between.  For example, with:
NY <> New York

A query for NY can return “New York Times”

It will not return “new company in York”, because the exact phrase “New York” isn’t present

This also means that “New” and “York” appear in different attributes within the same records, the record wil not be returned.
An alternative solution here would be to create a Rule to rewrite the query “NY” to "New York" instead of the synonym. Please check out our documentation on Rules here keeping the Replace Word consequence in mind. This way the query HD will match records that have both "High" and "Definition", without these words needing to be in the correct order.
Impact on ranking (exact criterion)
By default, multi-word synonyms are considered "less exact" than the original query terms and so score lower than their original words on the exact ranking criterion.
For example:
distant learning <> remote learning

Searching for “distant learning” and “remote learning” can return differently order results
This is because “remote learning” is treated as a synonym match and scores lower on the exact ranking criterion

To ensure that multi-word synonyms are treated the same as the original terms in ranking, update the alternativesAsExact setting to include multiWordsSynonym.
This allows both the original phrase and its synonym to score equally on the exact ranking criterion. 
For a full list of common synonym issues, troubleshooting topics and best practices, see our summary article here.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406975249297-Why-is-my-multi-word-synonym-not-matching-as-expected

answer.answer:
Algolia’s **distinct** setting deduplicates results by **grouping records that share the same value for a chosen attribute** (for example, a product/parent ID) and then **returning only the most relevant record from each group**.

## How distinct deduplication works
- You enable **distinct** on an attribute (e.g., `id`).
- When multiple records match a query but share the same value for that attribute (e.g., multiple variants of the same product), Algolia will **limit the number of returned records with that same attribute value**.
- In practice, that means you’ll see **one hit per group** (often “one product instead of all variants”), and the one shown is the **top-ranked/most relevant** among the grouped records.  
  Example described for Shopify: if “Product A” has 5 size variants that share the same product `id`, the results return only the single most relevant variant; the other variants with the same `id` aren’t displayed ([support article](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results)).

## Important side effect: facets can change
Because **facets are computed from the returned hits**, when results are deduplicated via distinct, your facet values may reflect only the attributes present on the remaining (most relevant) variant(s). This is why you can see facet/hit count quirks depending on how your faceting is configured (including “after distinct”) ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct), [Shopify example](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results)).

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
[Shopify] Can I display all variant facet values while deduplicating search results?
When you enable “Show products instead of variants” in the Algolia Shopify app, it activates the distinct feature on the id attribute on your Algolia index.The id  attribute represents the “product Id” attribute in your Shopify store, so the distinct feature will use this attribute to limit the number of returned records that contain the same value in that attribute. If your 'Product A' has 5 different sizes, your search will return only the single most relevant record; the other variants that share the same id ("product Id") will not be displayed. Facets will return the facet values that appear in the search results, so if the search results are deduplicated by the distinct parameter, your facets will only display the facet values of the returned variant. While this can be accomplished in non-Shopify implementations of Algolia by setting the facet filter as a non-afterDistinct faceting attribute (as described in this documentation), there is currently no custom hook to modify the default distinct behavior as it relates to faceting in the Algolia Shopify app.  Although we do not have an ETA at this time, our product team have added this functionality to the roadmap for a future release.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results

sources.text:
Why don't my facet counts match the number of hits for attributes set to 'after distinct'?
You may notice that when reviewing your index in the Algolia Dashboard, or on your InstantSearch frontend, that the number of hits returned by a query don't tally with the counts next to some of your facets.If this is the case, check whether the facet showing the incorrect count is set to 'after distinct' as an attribute for faceting.Setting 'after distinct' on an attribute for faceting means that facet counts will be calculated after distinct has been applied. Distinct is a method used to Group Results and is often employed in cases where you want to combine records into a single result, rather than displaying each variant as a separate hit. For example, you might have 5 records for the same t-shirt, one for each size the t-shirt is available in. The issue occurs when combining this with disjunctive faceting. Disjunctive faceting allows for the selection of multiple values within the same facet and to filter on them independently (e.g. size is Medium OR Large). It's the opposite of conjunctive faceting, where the values are treated as dependent (e.g. Medium AND Large).To allow for disjunctive faceting, InstantSearch makes two requests; one to retrieve the hits and one to retrieve the facets. The request to retrieve the facets, counts the deduplicated hits before they have been filtered, and this is what can lead to inconsistencies between the hits and the facet counts.This is a limitation of InstantSearch and the only way to ensure consistent counts under these circumstances is to use conjunctive faceting instead. This can be enabled within your InstantSearch refinementList widget by changing the operator parameter value to 'AND'. There's no way to do this within the Algolia Dashboard. There are several scenarios that could lead to incorrect or inconsistent facet counts. If this article doesn't relate to your issue, please have a look at the below article that describes other issues related to this topic: Facet and hit count issues checklist
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct

answer.answer:
The `distinct` setting deduplicates by **grouping records that share the same value of a chosen attribute**, and then returning **only a limited number of records per group**—often just **the single most relevant record** for that group. For example, if a product has multiple variant records (like size variants) that all share the same product id, `distinct` can make the search return only one of those variants instead of all of them.  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results

## What it does (conceptually)
- `distinct` is used to **Group Results** so you can “combine records into a single result, rather than displaying each variant as a separate hit.”  
  Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct

## What you’ll observe in results (example behavior)
- When distinct is applied on an attribute like a Shopify “product Id” (`id`), and a product has multiple variants, the search results will show **only the most relevant variant**; other variants with the same `id` won’t be displayed.  
  Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results

## Important side effect: facets can look “missing” or inconsistent
- Because facets “return the facet values that appear in the search results,” if results are deduplicated by `distinct`, facets may only show values from the returned (non-removed) variants.  
  Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results
- If a facet is configured as **“after distinct”**, that means “facet counts will be calculated after distinct has been applied.”  
  Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct

What attribute are you using (or planning to use) for `distinct` (for example, a product ID, model number, or something else)?

answer.answer:
Configure **Searchable Attributes** by explicitly listing which fields Algolia should search—and in what priority order—so relevance is predictable and consistent across your dataset. Leaving it empty can work, but it can also lead to inconsistent behavior when records don’t share the same schema ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes)).

## What “Searchable Attributes” controls
**Searchable Attributes** is the list of record attributes Algolia will look into when matching a query. The order matters because it helps Algolia **prioritize important fields** (for example, `name` over `description`) ([why this matters](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes)).

## Recommended way to configure it
### 1) Don’t rely on the default if your schema varies
By default, if you don’t configure Searchable Attributes, **all attributes are searchable**. However, if some attributes are missing from some records, leaving this unset can make it *seem* like Algolia “isn’t searching everything” ([explanation](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes)).

### 2) Explicitly list the attributes you want searched
In the Dashboard (Configuration tab) or via API, **define your searchable attributes** to:
- Control which fields are searchable
- Prioritize the most important fields
- Ensure consistent behavior across queries ([recommended solution](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes))

### 3) If an attribute isn’t in the Dashboard dropdown, add it manually
The Dashboard’s attribute dropdowns are built from a sample of the **first 10 records** returned by an empty query. If your attribute isn’t present in those records, it won’t appear—but you can still type it in and add it manually ([how it works](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8014088474513-Why-don-t-all-of-my-attributes-appear-in-the-configuration-drop-down-inputs-on-the-Algolia-Dashboard)).

## Common related adjustments (when needed)
### Limit searchable attributes at query time (optional)
If you want to override the index-wide setting for a specific search, you can restrict searchable fields at query time using `restrictSearchableAttributes` ([support note](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7642016208529-How-can-I-limit-the-attributes-that-are-searchable-at-query-time)).

### Watch out for very long text fields
For **exact phrase** matching, Algolia only keeps word positions for the **first 1,000 words** of an attribute—so phrases deep in long text may not match as expected. Consider splitting long content into smaller records and/or prioritizing the most important attributes in Searchable Attributes ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4420156558481-Why-does-an-exact-phrase-query-fail-even-though-the-phrase-exists-in-my-record)).

## Learn-by-doing resource
Algolia Academy has a dedicated module on this topic: [Configuring Searchable Attributes](https://academy.algolia.com/training/019465d6-0539-7661-8640-9b0c3656c165).

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Why isn't Algolia searching all my records' attributes?
If Algolia doesn’t appear to be searching across every attribute in your records, the issue may be related to your Searchable Attributes configuration. This article explains how Algolia determines which attributes are searchable, and why explicitly configuring them can help ensure consistent behavior.Default behavior: all attributes are searchableBy default, when you create an index in Algolia and don't configure Searchable Attributes, every attribute in every record is searchable. This means you can start using search right away without any manual setup.So why might it not work as expected?If your records don’t all share the same schema, for example, if some objects are missing certain fields, leaving Searchable Attributes empty can lead to inconsistent search behavior. Algolia may not reliably search an attribute that isn't present in every record, making it seem like some attributes are being ignored.Recommended solution: define your searchable attributesTo avoid unpredictability, especially when your data structure varies between records, we strongly recommend configuring the Searchable Attributes explicitly in the Configuration tab of the Dashboard (or via the API). This lets you:
Control which fields are searchable
Prioritize more important attributes
Ensure consistent behavior across all queries
Summary
All attributes are searchable by default, but only when all records share the same schema.
If your records vary, leaving Searchable Attributes empty can cause unexpected results.
To ensure consistent and predictable search behavior, explicitly define your Searchable Attributes.

sources.text:
Why does an exact phrase query fail even though the phrase exists in my record?
If an exact phrase appears in your data but doesn’t match in search, one possible cause is the position of that phrase within the attribute.Exact phrase matching depends on word positions. Algolia only keeps the positions of the first 1,000 words of each attribute. If the phrase appears beyond that point, the engine may not be able to match it as an exact phrase, even when the text is present in the record.This usually affects large text fields such as long descriptions, article bodies, or page content.To reduce the impact of this limitation, the best approach is to index smaller records so the searchable content is split into shorter sections. For long documents or pages, this often means breaking the content into chunks instead of storing everything in one large attribute.It can also help to review your searchableAttributes configuration so the most important content is prioritized in search. This won’t remove the 1,000-word position limit for phrase matching, but it can improve how relevant content is ranked and matched overall.If your use case depends heavily on exact phrase queries, check whether the phrase is located deep inside a long attribute. If it is, restructuring the indexed content into smaller records is usually the most reliable solution.Resources
Tokenization
Index long pages
searchableAttributes

If you're not seeing certain attributes available to select from the dropdown menus of Attributes for faceting, Searchable attributes, Custom Ranking, or similar configuration sections in the Algolia Dashboard, it’s likely due to how the attribute list is generated.How attribute drop down inputs are populatedThe attributes available for selection are based on a "records sample" taken from your index. Specifically, this sample is built from the first 10 records returned by an empty search (i.e. a search with no query). If an attribute doesn't appear in any of those 10 records, it won’t show up in the dropdown list, even if it exists in other records.Adding attributes that don't appearYou can still add any attribute manually, even if it’s not listed:

[Shopify] How can I make the "named_tags" and/or "named_tags_names" attribute values searchable?
When you add a tag (ie: rainbow), it will be indexed in the tags attribute. 
 
When you add a key:value tags (ie: color: rainbow),  it will be indexed in the named_tags attribute, and the named_tags_names attribute will hold the property names (in this case, color). 
 
If you'd like the named_tags and/or named_tags_names values to be searchable, you can add these attributes as searchableAttributes in your Algolia index configuration.

Why are the search results in Algolia different from other search engines, extensions, and integrations?
If you're switching to Algolia after previously using a search engine that's built-in to your website or integrated into your website by using a third-party extension, then you might wonder why the ordering of your results may be different after adding your records and configuring the index. There are a few reasons why the order of the search results may be different.We use a different relevance criteriaTo order the search results, we use a ranking criteria to define relevance. For our algorithm, the search results rank based on the following criteria:
Typo
Geo
Words
Filters
Proximity
Attribute
Exact
Custom
Attribute and proximity combinations
You can find out more about what this criteria means in our documentation: Ranking Critiera. Searchable Attributes may not include every attribute you'd likeThe Searchable Attributes list is for adding attributes you'd like the search engine to search through while retrieving results. Please check that list to ensure that all preferred attributes are included. Rules, Dynamic Re-Ranking, and Personalization could be appliedBeyond applying the relevance criteria while ranking results, other factors could be at play due to an index's configuration. If you're using Rules, Dynamic Re-Ranking, or Personalization, then your search results' ranking could be affected. You can find out more about how these features are applied to ranking here: Effect on Relevance. While we can't comment on how other search engines and extensions rank their results, we hope this article gives you information regarding how Algolia ranks your search results.

answer.answer:
Configure **Searchable Attributes** by **explicitly listing the fields you want Algolia to search**, rather than leaving it empty—especially if your records don’t all share the exact same schema. Leaving it empty can lead to **inconsistent behavior** where some attributes appear not to be searched. https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes

## What happens if you don’t configure Searchable Attributes
- **Default behavior:** if you don’t configure Searchable Attributes, **every attribute in every record is searchable**, so you can start searching without setup. https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes
- **Where it can go wrong:** if your records **vary in schema** (some objects missing certain fields), leaving Searchable Attributes empty can cause **unpredictable results**—Algolia may not reliably search an attribute that isn’t present in every record. https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes

## The “right way”: explicitly define them
Algolia recommends explicitly configuring Searchable Attributes (Dashboard **Configuration** tab, or via API) so you can:  
- **Control which fields are searchable**  
- **Prioritize more important attributes**  
- **Ensure consistent behavior across all queries**  
https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes

## If you can’t find an attribute in the Dashboard dropdown
If an attribute doesn’t show up in the Dashboard dropdown for Searchable Attributes, it may be because the dropdown is built from a **sample of the first 10 records** returned by an empty search. If the attribute isn’t in those records, it won’t appear. You can still **type the attribute name exactly** and add it manually. https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8014088474513-Why-don-t-all-of-my-attributes-appear-in-the-configuration-drop-down-inputs-on-the-Algolia-Dashboard

What kind of data are you searching (for example: ecommerce products, docs/articles, marketplace listings), and do all your records share the same set of fields?

Why isn't Algolia searching all my records' attributes?
If Algolia doesn’t appear to be searching across every attribute in your records, the issue may be related to your Searchable Attributes configuration. This article explains how Algolia determines which attributes are searchable, and why explicitly configuring them can help ensure consistent behavior.Default behavior: all attributes are searchableBy default, when you create an index in Algolia and don't configure Searchable Attributes, every attribute in every record is searchable. This means you can start using search right away without any manual setup.So why might it not work as expected?If your records don’t all share the same schema, for example, if some objects are missing certain fields, leaving Searchable Attributes empty can lead to inconsistent search behavior. Algolia may not reliably search an attribute that isn't present in every record, making it seem like some attributes are being ignored.Recommended solution: define your searchable attributesTo avoid unpredictability, especially when your data structure varies between records, we strongly recommend configuring the Searchable Attributes explicitly in the Configuration tab of the Dashboard (or via the API). This lets you:
Control which fields are searchable
Prioritize more important attributes
Ensure consistent behavior across all queries
Summary
All attributes are searchable by default, but only when all records share the same schema.
If your records vary, leaving Searchable Attributes empty can cause unexpected results.
To ensure consistent and predictable search behavior, explicitly define your Searchable Attributes.

sources.text:
Handling Multi-Language Indices in Algolia with Query Suggestions
When building a search experience for a multilingual audience, it's essential to ensure that users receive results and suggestions in the correct language. This is especially important in countries like Switzerland or Belgium, where multiple languages are spoken.
In this article, we’ll walk through a practical solution for managing multi-language records in Algolia, including how to configure query suggestions so users always see relevant results in their preferred language.
 
The Use Case
Let’s say your product is available in countries that support multiple languages. For example:


Switzerland: French, German, Italian


In this case, we can store all language versions of each product within the same index, and filter at query time based on the user's language.
 
The Data Structure
Each product is duplicated per language. So if a product is available in English and Italian, for example, it would be indexed twice - once in each language.
 
[ {  "name": "Borsa",  "description": "Descrizione in italiano",  "language": "it" }, {  "name": "Bag",  "description": "Description in English",  "language": "en" }]
Each record:


Is translated in all searchable attributes (like name, description).


Contains a language attribute to identify the language.


Yes, this increases the number of records — but since each record is lighter, search performance remains fast.
 
Filtering Results by Language
To ensure users only see results in their preferred language, add a simple filter when searching:

index.search(query, {  filters: 'language:it'})




This guarantees that only Italian results are returned.
You can also tag your queries with the user’s language for analytics tracking:


analyticsTags: ['language.it']

This will allow filtering your analytics data by language.



 
Query Suggestions
Algolia’s Query Suggestions can also be configured to return results in the correct language.
To do this:


Add the language as a category attribute when indexing query suggestions.


The number of categories to show (under Show top) must be equal or higher to the number of languages available in the index. This makes sure that suggestions are not excluded by any language:

Apply a language filter at query time:




querySuggestionsIndex.search(query, {  filters: 'source_index_name.facets.exact_matches.language.value:it'})






This ensures that only Italian suggestions are shown when the user is browsing in Italian.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/34407082474769-Handling-Multi-Language-Indices-in-Algolia-with-Query-Suggestions

sources.text:
What's the difference between Not Searchable, Searchable and Filter-Only facets?
When setting your attributesForFaceting, you can configure each attribute in one of the following ways:

Not searchable (default): if you want the attribute to be used as a facet
Searchable: if you want users to be able to search for facet values

Filter only: if you want the attribute to be used only for filtering (not faceting)

Not Searchable vs Searchable
When an attribute is declared as "not-searchable" or "searchable", it can be used for both faceting and filtering.
The term 'searchable', refers to the ability to search within the facet values themselves, rather than including the facet values as searchable terms when a query is run. This setting is useful for when you want users to be able to search for specific facet values.
For instance, a facet attribute may have thousands of different values making it impossible to display them all in your UI. By setting the facet as searchable, users can look up specific values within that attribute without the need to create a separate index. It means you can still display the most common values first, whilst allowing users to search for more options.
In the example below, category_page_id has been set as searchable, so users can search for the desired value without having to scroll through all 42 of the attribute's values.
 


Filtering 

When you need to filter on an attribute, such as color, but don’t need to calculate or display the number of times each color (eg, color:green, color:blue etc ) appears in your records, then use filterOnly.
This setting reduces the index size and improves search speed. You can find more on the difference between filtering and faceting in our docs here.

How to declare attributes for faceting in the dashboard
How to declare attributes for faceting in the API
How to show all facet values (including ones with no hits)

answer.answer:
I couldn’t find any relevant Algolia documentation or guides in the indexed Algolia corpus about implementing “backend search” specifically with **React InstantSearch** (including backend/proxy or SSR patterns), so I don’t want to guess and potentially mislead you.

## What I can do next (to point you to the right approach)
If you tell me what you mean by “backend search,” I can try a more targeted lookup:
- **Proxying search requests through your server** (to hide keys, apply auth, enforce filters)?
- **Server-side rendering (SSR)** React InstantSearch (e.g., for SEO/initial load)?
- **Using Algolia from your backend only** (and not from the browser), then rendering results in React?

Also helpful:
- Your stack (Next.js / Remix / CRA / custom SSR)
- Whether you need **per-user access control** (multi-tenant data) or just want to **hide credentials**

## If you have per-user access control
Algolia’s typical pattern is to keep the **Admin API key** on the server and generate **secured API keys** (or otherwise restrict search) for the frontend; if that’s your goal, I can look up the exact recommended docs once you confirm.

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

React InstantSearch is a **frontend** UI library; if you want “backend search” (search requests executed from your server), you’ll typically implement it as **server-side rendering (SSR)** for your InstantSearch experience. Algolia has training specifically on **InstantSearch + SSR (and routing) with React**: https://academy.algolia.com/training/0191df91-8892-7dba-91ae-4d048587454d

## What “backend search” maps to with React InstantSearch
### 1) Server-Side Rendering (SSR) with InstantSearch (recommended path for “backend”)
The React InstantSearch UI renders on the server, which means the search UI is generated server-side (commonly for SEO/performance). Algolia’s InstantSearch SSR + routing training covers this approach:  
https://academy.algolia.com/training/0191df91-8892-7dba-91ae-4d048587454d

### 2) Calling Algolia from your backend with an API client (server-side integration)
If your goal is to run queries from the server (outside of InstantSearch UI), Algolia provides server-side API clients (example called out: **PHP client**) and also React InstantSearch for client-side UI. This support article summarizes that split and links to the clients/libraries:  
https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37799063630609-Does-Algolia-offer-a-support-PrestaShop-extension

## Quick clarification so I can point you to the right implementation
When you say “backend search,” do you mean **SSR for your React InstantSearch UI**, or do you mean a **server-only endpoint (proxy) that runs searches and returns JSON to your React app**?

answer.answer:
**NeuralSearch** is Algolia’s AI-powered search capability designed to make results more relevant by providing **explainability into why items rank where they do**, including visibility into the ranking factors influencing each result ([NeuralSearch Explainability](https://academy.algolia.com/training/7ea73420-0247-11ef-90fa-02a84b0442e5)).

## What NeuralSearch is
NeuralSearch focuses on helping you understand *how* and *why* results are returned by exposing transparency features in the dashboard, including:
- **Transparent search result pages**
- **Ranking factor explanations**
- **Detailed insights per result**  
([NeuralSearch Explainability](https://academy.algolia.com/training/7ea73420-0247-11ef-90fa-02a84b0442e5))

## How NeuralSearch ranks results (based on available sources)
From the retrieved content, Algolia describes NeuralSearch ranking in terms of **ranking factors** and **explainability**—i.e., it provides tooling to **inspect what influenced the rank** of each hit and understand result ordering ([NeuralSearch Explainability](https://academy.algolia.com/training/7ea73420-0247-11ef-90fa-02a84b0442e5)).

However, the search results I retrieved **don’t include a public doc page** that breaks down the exact ranking algorithm/mechanics (for example, how different signals are weighted) beyond this explainability overview.

## Where to look next
If you want the precise details of the ranking logic and which factors are used/weighted, the best next step is to consult Algolia’s official documentation and support resources:
- Docs: https://www.algolia.com/doc

answer.answer:
NeuralSearch is an association-based AI model that improves query understanding so results can match not just the keywords in the query, but also the *concept* of what the searcher is looking for. It’s trained on your own click and conversion events and uses vectors to rank results.  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

## How NeuralSearch ranks results
NeuralSearch ranks using a **neural score** that can be made up of:
- a **keyword score**
- a **semantic score**
- or **both**  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

### 1) Keyword score (keyword search)
- Represents how well a record matches the query via **keyword search**, compared to other keyword-retrieved results.
- “At the base of this score is Algolia’s tie-breaking algorithm.”
- A record only has a keyword score **if keyword search finds it**.  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

### 2) Semantic score (vector search)
- Represents how well a record matches the query via **vector search**, compared to other vector-retrieved results.
- “At the basis of this score is neural hash similarity.”
- A record only has a semantic score **if vector search finds it**.  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

### 3) Neural score (combined relevance)
- Combines the keyword score and the semantic score.
- Measures relevance **relative to the other records in the result set**—it answers: *“Against this query, compared to these other results, how relevant is this result?”*  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

## Why it can be hard to “explain” one exact position
Because there are many criteria involved in ranking, “it’s not possible to definitively describe why a result is ranked on a specific position.”  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

What are you trying to optimize for with NeuralSearch (for example, better handling of vague queries, synonyms/concepts, or improving ranking based on click/conversion behavior)?

What’s the impact of changing my Algolia index name?
Changing the name of an Algolia index can have significant consequences on your Algolia implementation and some key features.The most important consideration is that any code, configuration, or integration referencing the original index name must be updated. Otherwise, this change can lead to unexpected behavior or application errors. When renaming an index the below warning message will appear. This relates to the impact on Access and Analytics. In addition to this, renaming will also have an impact on frontend integrations, events tracking, and any features dependent on index-specific events, as these are not transferred to the new index name.Full details on the various impacts can be found below:

Access:
Within Team Member permissions, it's possible to restrict which indices users have access to, for example, you may declare dev_*, staging_* to grant a user access to all indices with a common prefix. Therefore, if you change the name of an index that you have specified a user has access to, you will need to ensure you update the name in the user's index permission settings.


Frontend Integrations:
Any frontend code, integrations, or connectors that reference the old index name will need to be updated to use the new index name. Otherwise, this change can lead to unexpected behavior or application errors.


Analytics: 
Analytics data is tied to an index by its name, not by an internal identifier. Analytics events are collected and stored on a separate analytics server from the search indices, and the index name is used as the link between them. As a result, renaming an index breaks this association, and any historical analytics data collected under the old name will no longer be accessible under the new one.


Event Tracking: 
Events are tied to the specific index name. If you change the index name, you cannot transfer historical events to the new index. More information in this article: Can I transfer events to a different index?



Dynamic Re-Ranking: 

Algolia’s Dynamic Re-Ranking feature operates at the index level, which means it cannot draw upon insights from multiple indices simultaneously. After changing the index name, Dynamic Re-Ranking loses access to the historical event data that powers optimized ranking. As a result, the new index must wait until enough events have been collected before the feature will work again.
The original configuration for the index won't match anymore upon index renaming. Any configuration will need to be re-created for this feature.
When renaming an index, the enableReRanking index settings will be set to false.



Query Categorization (QCat): 

QCat uses historical queries and click data. Renaming the index breaks the link to the event data. The new index must wait until enough events have been collected before the feature will work again. 
The original configuration for the index won't match anymore upon index renaming. Any configuration will need to be re-created for this feature.



Dynamic Synonym Suggestions: 

Suggestions are based on search and click analytics. Renaming or copying an index breaks the connection to historical data.
The original configuration for the index won't match anymore upon index renaming. Any configuration will need to be re-created for this feature.
Every declined synonym will be lost and they will potentially be seen in "pending" again. Historically accepted synonyms will still exist on the renamed index, but they won't be visible in the DSS section of the dashboard



Query Suggestions:
If the index is set as the source index for a Query Suggestions index, the configuration for that QS index should be updated to reflect the new index name.


Neural Search:
Neural training data is index-specific. A renamed/copied index loses link to prior data.


Virtual Replicas (VRs):
If you copy an index to another app and rename it, VRs will also be copied and remain as VRs of their respective new primary indices. The relationship is preserved, but the names will reflect the new primary index. More information in this article: Do virtual replicas stay connected when I copy indices with new names to another app?
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37490688595217-What-s-the-impact-of-changing-my-Algolia-index-name

sources.text:
What happens if we set analytics as false for a query but set clickAnalytics as true? Will click analytics still work and set a queryID for searches so I can send events?
Yes, the analytics and click analytics search parameters are separate.If you set analytics as false (which means that the search will not appear in your analytics) you can still set clickAnalytics as true, and use the search to send events.However this click event will not be tracked in your analytics, because there will be no search in your analytics to link it back to. As there will be no search information, the events can be used for Algolia Recommend or Personalization but they will not work with NeuralSearch, Dynamic Re-Ranking, Query Categorisation or A/B Testing which all require events to be linked to a search.

sources.text:
Privacy manifest
Note: If your Algolia implementation collects tracking data, Apple requires you to add a privacy manifest that describes your reason and method for collecting tracking data.
 
What is a privacy manifest?
A privacy manifest is a file in your Xcode project that describes the reason your app and third-party SDKs collect data, along with their data-collection methods. Each of your third-party SDKs that track data require their own privacy manifest. When you create your app’s privacy report, these privacy manifest files are automatically aggregated into a single report.
 
Declaring your privacy manifest
Step 1: Review current policies
Before you begin, review your Algolia SDK’s current data collection policies and API usage within your application. Determine which elements of your application collect data and what APIs you are using.
 
See the following pages for more documentation about the collection of end-user data by Algolia’s SDK’s. Please note that most of the time, end-user data collection is needed for Algolia features to operate (e.g. Recommend, Re-Ranking, Personalisation, Neuralsearch).

User token
Event types
Get started with click and conversion events

 
Step 2: Declare data collection practices
We have provided template privacy manifests for both the InstantSearch iOS Library and the Swift API Client. You can start with these templates to ensure you cover all required aspects.
By default, the Algolia SDK does not collect tracking data. However, depending on your Algolia SDK configuration, you may be required to list Algolia-specific data in your app’s privacy manifest.
 
Step 3: Customize privacy manifest
Edit the provided files to reflect the specific data collection and API usage of your applications. Ensure each entry is clearly justified and aligns with Apple’s guidelines.
 
Step 4: Integrate privacy manifest into application
Once your privacy manifest is customized and complete, integrate into your iOS project. This involves adding your files to your project settings and ensuring it is included in your build process.

answer.answer:
**Vector search** is a way to retrieve and rank results by comparing **vectors** (numerical representations of meaning) rather than only matching exact keywords. In practice, it helps search understand the **concept** behind a query and find semantically related items even if they don’t share the same words.

## How vector search works (high level)
- Content (documents, products, etc.) and queries are represented as **vectors**.
- The search system finds the nearest vectors (most similar meaning) and uses that similarity to rank results.

## Vector search in Algolia (NeuralSearch context)
Algolia’s **NeuralSearch** combines classic keyword relevance with semantic relevance. In Algolia’s explanation:
- A result can get a **keyword score** when it matches via traditional keyword search.
- A result can get a **semantic score** when it matches via **vector search**.
- A final **neural score** can combine both to rank results overall ([How does NeuralSearch rank results?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results)).

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

answer.answer:
Vector search is a way to retrieve results by comparing **vectors** (numeric representations) so you can match on the **concept** of what someone is looking for—not just exact keyword matches. In Algolia’s NeuralSearch context, this shows up as results that can match the *meaning* of the query, not only the words in it.  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

## How it fits into NeuralSearch
NeuralSearch uses vectors to help rank results, and it produces a **neural score** that can include:
- a **keyword score** (from keyword search), and/or
- a **semantic score** (from **vector search**)  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

## What “semantic score” means here
When a record is found via vector search, it can get a **semantic score**, which represents how well it matches the query **compared to other vector-retrieved results**. The article notes this is based on **neural hash similarity**.  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results

What are you trying to use vector search for—ecommerce product search, documentation/content search, or something else?

sources.text:
How can I make sure Algolia NeuralSearch is working correctly?
You can review our documentation on getting started with NeuralSearch.Broadly, you'll want to ensure that:

You are on an eligible plan (either the Elevate or Elevate Ecomm plan).
You have enabled NeuralSearch on your index as per this guide.
You've configured your searchable attributes as per this guide and your relevance as per this guide. This ensures that queries not yet generating a semantic score will still generate a relevant keyword score.  
The data structure of your record is a good use case for NeuralSearch (see "Data Structure" section below).
You are sending events (see "Events" section below).
Your use case doesn't conflict with any of the known limitations (see "Limitations" section below).

 
Data Structure
Here's an example of a record structure and settings that would work with NeuralSearch:

 
 
NeuralSearch is not the best solution for search implementations that only search on SKUs or other exact matches.
 
Events

Have you implemented events?
Are your events meeting the prerequisite requirements for NeuralSearch? The requirements for the number of events may change, but the latest information can be found in this documentation. 
Are your events valid?

Use the Events Debugger to assess whether or not your events are valid. Valid events will have a green dot in the "Status" column as shown below:




You should also make sure that you aren't sending bot-generated events. For more information, please refer to our guide on how to deal with bots.
 
Limitations 
NeuralSearch currently has some feature differences and limitations, including:

Multi-region DSN is not currently supported.
Index size is limited to 100 GB.
Application size is limited to 1 TB.
You cannot use the deleteBy operation. Use the delete method instead, which is significantly more performant.

Custom dictionaries are not currently available.
Real time logs are not currently available.

Vault is not currently available
There are some internal indexing differences.
NeuralSearch is not configurable for HIPAA-Enabled Service Features
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/15032552758161-How-can-I-make-sure-Algolia-NeuralSearch-is-working-correctly

Algolia supports ecommerce and retail search by providing a **hosted search and discovery platform** designed to deliver **fast, relevant, and customizable** experiences for online stores and retail applications ([Support article](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406975269649-What-can-I-do-with-Algolia)).

## What Algolia enables for ecommerce & retail
From the content available in Algolia’s support resources, Algolia is positioned to power:
- **Search experiences** for product catalogs and retail content
- **Discovery experiences** that help users find what they want quickly
- More advanced experiences such as **agentic and generative** search flows, depending on your implementation needs ([overview](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406975269649-What-can-I-do-with-Algolia))

## Why it’s used in retail contexts
Algolia highlights these themes as core to where it fits well:
- **Lightning-fast responses**
- **Relevant results**
- **Highly customizable behavior and ranking**
- Applicability across **e-commerce and retail** specifically, among other industries ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406975269649-What-can-I-do-with-Algolia))

## Notes on scope
In the retrieved content for this question, I only found a high-level support overview (not detailed, feature-by-feature ecommerce documentation). If you share what platform you’re on (Shopify, Salesforce Commerce Cloud, Magento/Adobe Commerce, custom headless, etc.) and what you’re trying to improve (onsite search, category browsing, recommendations, etc.), I can point you to more specific docs/resources.

What can I do with Algolia?
Algolia is a data retrieval and search-as-a-service (SaaS) solution that provides and powers agentic, generative, and search experiences across e-commerce and retail, documentation and knowledge base, media and publishing, and enterprise search and beyond. Where lightning fast, relevant, and customizable search and discovery is paramount.If providing fast, relevant, search, agentic and generative experiences is essential for your website or application, then you should give Algolia a try. Algolia is the only search engine built from the ground up for these kinds of experience. You can have a look at our features, and we’d be happy to show you a demo.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406975269649-What-can-I-do-with-Algolia

answer.answer:
Algolia supports ecommerce and retail search by providing a search-as-a-service platform designed to power “lightning fast, relevant, and customizable” search and discovery experiences for ecommerce and retail use cases, including agentic and generative experiences. https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406975269649-What-can-I-do-with-Algolia

## What that means in practice (for ecommerce/retail)
From the Algolia content I have here, ecommerce and retail support includes:
- **Search + discovery experiences** (positioned specifically for ecommerce and retail, among other industries). https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406975269649-What-can-I-do-with-Algolia
- **Agentic and generative experiences** as part of those ecommerce/retail experiences. https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406975269649-What-can-I-do-with-Algolia

## What products/features are explicitly mentioned as supported (relevant to commerce)
The Algolia Support Team lists support for a number of products commonly used in ecommerce implementations, including:
- **Merchandising**: *Merchandising Studio and the Visual Editor* https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10960300066577-What-Algolia-Products-are-supported-by-the-Algolia-Support-Team
- **Recommend**: *Algolia Recommend* https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10960300066577-What-Algolia-Products-are-supported-by-the-Algolia-Support-Team
- **Personalisation**: *Algolia personalisation* https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10960300066577-What-Algolia-Products-are-supported-by-the-Algolia-Support-Team
- **Click & conversion analytics**: *Insights REST API, Insights API Client* https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10960300066577-What-Algolia-Products-are-supported-by-the-Algolia-Support-Team
- **A/B Testing**: *Algolia A/B testing* (with the note that support can’t help analyze results) https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10960300066577-What-Algolia-Products-are-supported-by-the-Algolia-Support-Team
- **Commerce integrations** mentioned as supported: *Algolia for Shopify*, *Algolia for Commercetools*, *Algoliasearch Magento 2*, and *Algolia for Salesforce Commerce Cloud B2C (SFCC)* https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10960300066577-What-Algolia-Products-are-supported-by-the-Algolia-Support-Team
- **UI libraries**: *Autocomplete.js* and *InstantSearch* libraries (JS/React/Vue) https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10960300066577-What-Algolia-Products-are-supported-by-the-Algolia-Support-Team

## What I don’t have in the retrieved content
I don’t have anything in the results above that explains *how* these ecommerce features work (for example: ranking strategy, faceting, category pages/PLPs, on-site search UX patterns, etc.)—only that Algolia supports ecommerce/retail search experiences and lists specific products/integrations that are supported.

What ecommerce platform are you on today (Shopify, Magento, Commercetools, SFCC, or something else)?
