answer.answer:
Use **`filters`** for *all “hard” filtering logic* (especially numeric, boolean, and ranges), use **`facetFilters`** for *facet-based hard filters* you want to behave like UI facets, and use **`optionalFilters`** when you *don’t want to exclude results*—you just want to **boost** records matching certain attribute/value pairs.

## `filters` (hard filter expression)
### When to use
- When you need a **single, expressive filter string** (AND/OR/NOT, parentheses, ranges, numeric comparisons).
- When you’re filtering on **numeric attributes** (price ranges, rating thresholds) or combinations that aren’t strictly “facet selection”.

### Notes
- Any attribute you filter on must be present in records (Algolia can’t filter on missing/null attributes) ([support note](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13032145848337-Do-I-need-to-replicate-every-piece-of-data-across-each-record)).

## `facetFilters` (hard filters specifically for facets)
### When to use
- When you’re applying **faceted navigation** selections (e.g., `brand:Apple`, `category:Shoes`) and you want those selections to be treated as facet filters.
- Common in search UIs where users click facets; it maps naturally to “selected facets”.

### Notes
- If you have rules that dynamically apply facet filters, conflicts can arise if the same query matches multiple facet attributes (example and behavior described here: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9116630370961-How-does-Algolia-handle-conflicting-dynamic-filter-rules-when-the-same-query-string-value-appears-in-multiple-attributes).

## `optionalFilters` (soft filters / boosting)
### When to use
- When you want to **promote** records matching some attribute/value, but still return other records too.
- Useful for cases like “query matches a brand *or* a model; boost both” instead of forcing only one facet filter.

### Example use case (from support)
To avoid choosing only one conflicting facet filter, you can use `optionalFilters` to boost both matches (while still showing the rest afterward) ([example](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9116630370961-How-does-Algolia-handle-conflicting-dynamic-filter-rules-when-the-same-query-string-value-appears-in-multiple-attributes)).

sources.text:
How does Algolia handle conflicting dynamic filter rules when the same query string value appears in multiple attributes?
If the same value appears in multiple filter attributes and this filter value is being used as a rule trigger condition to add filters dynamically, only one of the filtering rules -- whichever has the lower ruleID -- will be triggered.
For example, my e-commerce sneaker site uses rules to apply dynamic filters to filter by brand or model whenever the user searches for a name that matches a brand or a model value. I have set up the following rules:

CONDITION: Query contains{facet:brand}--> CONSEQUENCE: Filter Facetbrand

CONDITION: Query contains {facet:model}--> CONSEQUENCE: Filter Facet model


If my site sells sneakers by a brand called "Jumpers" (brand: "Jumpers") and also sells sneakers from a different brand with the model Jumpers (model: "Jumpers"), this will initiate the precedence logic tie-breaking of the two conflicting rules. Because everything other than the facet filter is a tie in terms of the precedence logic, the model rule is applied simply because of the fact that the rule was created first and has a lower ruleID. The "Jumpers" brand sneakers will not appear in the results.
***
Although you cannot apply both facet filters simultaneously in this scenario, there are two potential workarounds, both of which would involve creating a new rule specifically for the query "Jumpers" that is generating the conflict:1. Create a rule with the condition that the query is "Jumpers". For the consequence, add the following query parameter:
{  "optionalFilters": [    "model:jumpers",    "brand:jumpers"  ]}
Although optionalFilters will also return results that don't match either of the filters, the parameter above will boost the Jumpers model records to the top, followed by all Jumpers brand records. Results that don't match the filters will appear in the results after both. You won't see the facets selected with this option.2. Create a rule with the condition that the query is "jumpers". There will be two sets of consequences. The first is to pin any records for "Jumpers" models to the top of the results. For the second, add the query parameter below:
{  "facetFilters": [    "brand:jumpers"  ]}
This second option is far less scalable, as you would need to manually re-pin the "Jumpers" model records anytime these records are updated or new "Jumpers" model records are added.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9116630370961-How-does-Algolia-handle-conflicting-dynamic-filter-rules-when-the-same-query-string-value-appears-in-multiple-attributes

answer.answer:
Use **`facetFilters`** when you want to *filter on facet attributes* (and have those refinements show up as selected facets), use **`filters`** for general “filter-by-attributes” constraints, and use **`optionalFilters`** when you want to *boost* records matching some attribute values without strictly excluding non-matching records.

## `facetFilters`: hard filtering tied to facets (UI refinements)
A Support article’s workaround shows `facetFilters` being applied as a strict filter (only records matching remain), e.g.:
```json
{ "facetFilters": [ "brand:jumpers" ] }
```
This is used specifically as a “Filter Facet …” consequence in Rules, and it’s treated as a true filter (not just a boost). Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9116630370961-How-does-Algolia-handle-conflicting-dynamic-filter-rules-when-the-same-query-string-value-appears-in-multiple-attributes

## `optionalFilters`: boosting (soft filtering)
That same article recommends `optionalFilters` when you can’t apply two conflicting facet filters at once. The key behavior it states:

- `optionalFilters` “will also return results that don't match either of the filters”
- but it “will boost … records to the top” (then non-matching results come after)
- “You won't see the facets selected with this option.”

Example given:
```json
{
  "optionalFilters": [
    "model:jumpers",
    "brand:jumpers"
  ]
}
```
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9116630370961-How-does-Algolia-handle-conflicting-dynamic-filter-rules-when-the-same-query-string-value-appears-in-multiple-attributes

## `filters`: general attribute filtering (prereq: attributes must exist)
From our Support guidance: if you want to use an attribute for **filtering**, **facet filters**, or **optional filters**, it must be present in your records, and Algolia “doesn’t support filtering on null values or missing attributes.”  
Source: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13032145848337-Do-I-need-to-replicate-every-piece-of-data-across-each-record

I don’t have a documentation hit in this search result set that precisely defines how `filters` differs from `facetFilters` in terms of syntax/behavior beyond the above, so I can’t go deeper on that comparison from retrieved content.

Which are you implementing right now: a faceted navigation UI (checkboxes/filters panel), or Rules that dynamically apply constraints based on the query?

A representative of Algolia is asking me to send them money (for a job opportunity etc)
We have been made aware that some individuals are falsely representing themselves as Algolia in order to trick individuals into sending them money with promises of a job or other opportunity/payment. If you have encountered any requests like this please be aware that:
We will never ask you to send us money in relation to any jobs or hiring at Algolia or ask you to wire money, buy gift cards, send cryptocurrency, or change billing details through informal channels such as Whatsapp or other messaging services.
All correspondence with our recruitment team will be from individuals with an @algolia.com email address or via LinkedIn where you can look for the verification badge on their LinkedIn profile
Our job postings may appear on third-party job boards, but the best way to apply safely is directly through our careers page https://www.algolia.com/careers

All correspondence with our Support team will be via our support center at https://support.algolia.com/ 
If you have any concerns:If you have any suspicions at all about a communication you have received then please do not hesitate to reach out to our support team who can confirm the validity of any request you have received. However, because such fraud does not operate through Algolia infrastructure or Algolia-controlled websites, we are not able to investigate these incidents from our side, recover funds, or take direct action against the perpetrators.What you can do next:
Preserve evidence: keep emails, messages, phone numbers, transaction details, wallet addresses, URLs, screenshots, and any files shared.
If money or account access is involved: contact your bank or payment provider immediately.
Report it to the relevant authority in your country (links below).
Online reporting portals:

United Kingdom: https://www.reportfraud.police.uk/

Germany (Onlinewache portal): https://portal.onlinewache.polizei.de/de/

United States: You can submit a report to the FBI’s Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) 3 at https://www.ic3.gov/

You may use a custom host and change the default hosts to which the API client connects. More example can be found from this documentation: https://www.algolia.com/doc/api-client/getting-started/customize/python/?client=python#use-a-custom-host

sources.text:
What do you do about General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)?
Algolia is in full GDPR compliance. We have adjusted our Terms of service to reflect this.
Privacy and security are critical to everything we do.
Security Status

Security audit (SOC2 Type 2) - Done

Mapping of security & privacy measures - Done CAIQ

Data store mapping - Done

Establishment of DPO - Done

Data Processing Addendum (DPA) - Done

Storage of customer’s DPO and security contacts - Done

Terms of Service with GDPR provisions - Done

Notification of customers about changes in conditions and DPA - Done


To discuss more details about GDPR, you can contact us at privacy@algolia.com.



 FAQ   What Is Algolia's Product Compliance? 



Common Questions & Answers
Is my service going to change because of GDPR?No, nothing changes in the scope of provided services.
Do you have GDPR compliance Terms of Service already?Yes, we posted our revised Terms of Service on April 25, 2018. Please see link
Will you send us a Data Processing Addendum?You can find our Data Processing Addendum (DPA) here. If you are an existing customer, then you are already covered under our online terms which incorporates the DPA by reference. If you don’t believe you’re already on our DPA, please contact your sales representative.
Will I be able to use Algolia in all regions around the world?Yes, Algolia complies with GDPR when providing its services all around the world.
What security measures do you have in place?You can take a look at our CAIQ 3.0.1 report for the Cloud Security Alliance where we declare the state of our security measures. Algolia is also SOC2 Type 2 audited organization and complies with all the Security, Availability and Confidentiality requirements.
What do you use personal data for?We only use personal data in accordance with the Agreement, your instructions and to provide the Services. We value your privacy, and we’ll do everything we can to protect it.
How long is the data retained?We retain the data for the duration of your service and after that for a maximum of 90 days.
Can I delete my personal data or that of my end users?Yes. Find out how to delete your personal data or how to delete your user’s data.
Where can I enter details about my Data Privacy Officer (DPO), and other such contact information?Our Dashboard has a page devoted to data privacy contact information, such as the name, email, and phone of the Data Privacy Officer (DPO), EU Representative, and Security contact. See our Data Privacy Contact FAQ.
Are you processing data outside of EU?Algolia, Inc. is a USA based company with subsidiaries in France and UK operating our services globally in more than 15 regions. Your data primarily stays in regions where you decide your data to reside. Logs of search queries and operations can be processed outside of the EU but always stay in a system respecting privacy and security.
Are there free usage units so that you can violate my privacy?No! The units are free because we like to support our (developer) community. There are no hidden intentions. We comply with GDPR with all our plans.
Can I discuss more details about GDPR and how you’re approaching it?Definitely! We like GDPR and like to discuss it. Contact us at privacy@algolia.com

Where are the clusters and servers located?
We are dedicated to offering high performance search and excellent user experience. For these reasons, we have deployed a distributed architecture with clusters around the world.
We currently have clusters in 16 different regions, spread over 70 different data centers worldwide:


US-East (Virginia): two different Equinix data centers in Ashburn & COPT DC-6 in Manassas (three independent, autonomous systems).


US-West (California): three different Equinix data centers in San Jose (three independent, autonomous systems).

Middle East: (DSN only) one data center in Dubai.

sources.text:
Do Query Suggestions increase the number of billable requests?
Query Suggestions are powered by a Query Suggestions index, and requests to this index are billed just like any other search request. However, whether they increase your total number of billable operations depends on how your frontend is implemented.Query Suggestions = Standard Search RequestsWhen your application sends a request to the Query Suggestions index, that request is counted as a billable search operation, the same as any request to a regular index. You can learn more in the article:How does Algolia count records and operationsOptimized Example: One request, multiple resultsIn many implementations (such as using the Autocomplete library), you can retrieve both Query Suggestions and search hits in a single request using Algolia's multipleQueries functionality under the hood.For example, in this CodeSandbox demo, the network tab shows only one request per keystroke, but results from both the hits index and the suggestions index are returned together. This means only one billable operation occurs per keystroke - even though you're querying two indices.Less optimized example: Separate requestsIn other implementations, such as in our guide on integrating Autocomplete with React InstantSearch Hooks, the Autocomplete menu handles suggestions, while InstantSearch handles hits in separate components. In this case, each component sends its own request, which leads to two billable operations per keystroke.Summary
Query Suggestions are billable search requests.
You can combine them with hits in a single request to avoid increasing your operation count.
Frontend implementation choices determine whether your request volume increases.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10291951587473-Do-Query-Suggestions-increase-the-number-of-billable-requests

sources.text:
Which queries are counted as part of the max QPS computations?
Algolia computes the maximum query per second (QPS) metrics from search queries only.Every time we receive a search query, we record the second it happened as part of the maximum QPS computations. Search queries include search operations for records, facets, synonyms, Rules, and Algolia Places. You can find out more about how we calculate the max QPS here.Note that search operations don't include the browse and deleteByQuery methods.

Why are query suggestions added using external analytics not appearing as suggested queries?
If you have added query suggestions via an external analytics source, and one or more of these queries is not appearing as expected, we recommend consulting your banned expressions list to ensure it has not been listed there.
Any queries listed in banned expressions will not be returned as suggestions.

sources.text:
Can I query additional indices if I don't have enough results?
Yes, you can use the multipleQueries method with the strategy parameter set to a value of stopIfEnoughMatches.
 
The strategy of the multipleQueries method can be one of the following values:


none: Execute the sequence of queries until the end. This is recommended when each query is of equal importance, meaning all records of all queries need to be returned.


stopIfEnoughMatches: Execute queries one by one, but stop as soon as the cumulated number of hits is at least hitsPerPage. This is recommended when each query is an alternative, and where, if the first returns enough records, there is no need to perform the remaining queries.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/14493252714513-Can-I-query-additional-indices-if-I-don-t-have-enough-results

answer.answer:
Algolia’s **typo tolerance** lets queries match records even when the user misspells terms (within configurable limits), so users still get relevant results instead of “no results.” I couldn’t find the specific doc pages in the Algolia public index right now to cite the exact setting names/behavior for turning it off per attribute or per word.

## How typo tolerance works (high level)
Typo tolerance generally:
- Matches words with a small **edit distance** (character insertions/deletions/substitutions)
- Applies different behavior for **short vs. long** words (typically stricter for short words)
- Can be configured at the **index** and sometimes **query** level (depending on settings)

## Can you turn it off for a specific attribute or words?
I can’t confirm from retrieved sources in the current index results whether Algolia supports:
- Disabling typo tolerance **for specific attributes**, and/or
- Disabling typo tolerance **for specific words**
(and what the exact configuration keys would be), so I don’t want to guess and risk giving you incorrect setting names.

## What to do next
- Check the **Typo tolerance** section in the Algolia docs (Index settings / Search parameters).
- If you tell me which product surface you’re using (Dashboard settings vs API parameters; Search vs Autocomplete), I can try another targeted lookup.

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

Algolia applies typo tolerance during matching so that results can still be returned even when the query contains typos. You *can* turn off typo tolerance for specific searchable attributes, but I don’t have anything in the retrieved content about disabling typo tolerance for specific **words**.

## Turn off typo tolerance for a specific attribute
If you want stricter matching on a given attribute, you can disable typo tolerance on that attribute using **`disableTypoToleranceOnAttribute(s)`**. This “will ensure that results matching on the attribute’s value with typos will not be returned.”  
https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11908802578449-How-can-I-get-a-strict-full-word-exact-match-for-a-specific-attribute

## Related: strict full-word matching (prefixing)
Separately (but often used together), you can also turn off prefix matching for some attributes using **`disablePrefixOnAttributes`**, so the attribute “is returned only if there is a full-word match.”  
https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11908802578449-How-can-I-get-a-strict-full-word-exact-match-for-a-specific-attribute

## Note about highlighting
Disabling typo tolerance on an attribute affects **matching**, but **highlighting is configured separately** (via `attributesToHighlight` and returned in `_highlightResult`), so turning off typo tolerance doesn’t change how highlights are returned.  
https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11258071469073-Why-isn-t-disableTypoToleranceOnAttributes-accounted-for-in-the-highlight

## What I don’t have in the retrieved content
I don’t have anything here that explains how to disable typo tolerance for specific **words** (only for attributes).

What attribute(s) are you trying to make strict (for example, SKU, brand, or part number)?

sources.text:
How can I get a strict full-word exact match for a specific attribute?
By default, Algolia is a prefix search based engine. Prefix search is usually better for relevance and performance. It enables the engine to start matching records based on partial words. This means that the engine doesn't wait for full-word match before displaying results.
However, if you want to get a more strict exact matching for a specific attribute you can turn off prefixing on some attributes using:

disablePrefixOnAttributes

This parameter will ensure that the attribute's value is returned only if there is a full-word match. You can find more information and use cases for this setting here.Additionally, if you want to only return exact matches, you may also want to disable typo tolerance for the attribute. This will ensure that results matching on the attributes value with typos will not be returned. 

disableTypoToleranceOnAttribute
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11908802578449-How-can-I-get-a-strict-full-word-exact-match-for-a-specific-attribute

However, if you want to get a more strict exact matching for a specific attribute you can turn off prefixing on some attributes using:

This parameter will ensure that the attribute's value is returned only if there is a full-word match. You can find more information and use cases for this setting here.Additionally, if you want to only return exact matches, you may also want to disable typo tolerance for the attribute. This will ensure that results matching on the attributes value with typos will not be returned.

answer.answer:
Algolia **Recommend** is Algolia’s product for generating **AI-driven recommendations** (for example, showing complementary or related items) based on user behavior and conversion signals. One of its models is **Frequently Bought Together (FBT)**, which recommends items that users tend to purchase together ([Recommend FAQs](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations)).

## What is Algolia Recommend?
Algolia Recommend lets you display recommendation carousels like “Frequently Bought Together” or “Related Products” by training machine-learning models on your **user interaction / conversion data** (for example purchase or add-to-cart events). The quality and coverage of recommendations depends on the events used and how much signal exists for each item ([coverage notes](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/6357583434257-Why-did-the-coverage-on-my-Frequently-Bought-Together-Recommend-model-decrease)).

## What the “Frequently Bought Together” model is (exactly)
The **FBT model** looks at **users’ conversion patterns** to recommend items that were bought together (or are likely to be bought together, depending on settings) ([model description + variants](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations)).

### Model variants
FBT has two variants you can choose from:
- **Relaxed (default)**: uses **collaborative filtering** to infer relationships from past purchases. Example: if users buy **A with B**, and **B with C**, the model may recommend **C for A** even if A and C weren’t purchased together directly ([example](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations)).
- **Strict**: recommends only items that were **converted together in the past** (no inferred pairings), usually resulting in **fewer** recommendations ([strict definition](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations)).

### What “conversion patterns” means in practice
- If you train on **addToCart** events, the model can be noisier (items added but not actually purchased).
- You can reduce noise by training using only **purchase** events via event customization ([event impact](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations)).

### Output behavior (how many items it returns)
By default, FBT returns **up to 3** recommendations per item, though you can configure higher limits (up to 30) in Recommend settings and then control what you display with `maxRecommendations` ([limits and configuration](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16288892997137-Why-am-I-only-see-3-recommendations-for-Frequently-Bought-Together-model), [why maxRecommendations > 3 may not change anything](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16990894904209-I-have-set-the-maxRecommendations-to-4-but-I-m-still-only-receiving-3-recommendations-in-the-front-end-Why-is-that)).

### Why there may be no “fallback” for FBT
Algolia notes that FBT recommendations **can’t be emulated** just by search parameters when there isn’t enough “bought together” data; in that case, it’s better to show another recommendation type (like related products) rather than forcing an FBT fallback ([fallback rationale](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11036775611537-Why-are-there-no-fallbackParameters-in-the-Frequently-Bought-Together-model)).

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Why are there no fallbackParameters in the Frequently Bought Together model?
You can’t usefallbackParameterswithFrequently Bought Togetheras it’s impossible to emulate based on search parameters alone. If there isn’t enough data forFrequently Bought Together, it’s better to show a different type of recommendation, for example, related products.
The Frequently Bought Together model only makes sense for items that are actually bought together. That’s why there are no fallback recommendations.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11036775611537-Why-are-there-no-fallbackParameters-in-the-Frequently-Bought-Together-model

sources.text:
Why am I only see 3 recommendations for Frequently Bought Together model?
By default, Algolia Recommend shows up to 3 recommendations for Frequently Bought Together.
 
To change this behaviour you can adjust the number by using the maxRecommendations
attribute.relatedProducts({  // ...  maxRecommendations: 5,});



You can also set the maximum number of recommendations per item to 30 in Recommend settings under step 3. Machine learning model.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16288892997137-Why-am-I-only-see-3-recommendations-for-Frequently-Bought-Together-model

sources.text:
Why am I seeing irrelevant products in Frequently Bought Together recommendations?
The Frequently Bought Together model looks at users' conversion patterns to recommend items that were either bought together or that might go well together. Model VariantsIt worth noting that our FBT model has two settings which you can choose between:Relaxed variant (default)The relaxed variant recommends items that are likely bought together. It uses collaborative filtering to infer relationships from past purchases. For example:
If users often buy product A with product B, and product B with product C
The model can suggest product C for product A, even if those two items weren’t bought together. 
This approach helps increase catalogue discovery, but can introduce inferred (less direct) pairings.Strict variantThe strict variant only recommends items that were converted together in the past. It doesn’t infer new pairings, so it typically returns fewer recommendations.Impact of EventsThe quality of recommendations depends on the conversion events used to train the model.If you are sending an addToCart conversion events, this can introduce some noise. For example, if a user adds an item to cart but does not complete the purchase.To reduce this "noise", (i.e. only shows products that were purchased together as opposed to added to cart together), you can use the event customization feature to train the model using only purchase events. This should result in the model being more relevant.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations

sources.text:
Why did the coverage on my Frequently Bought Together Recommend model decrease?
Frequently Bought Together does not maximize for coverage, it maximizes for quality recommendations. In this case, it is likely due to the fact that the events have changed for products, meaning customer behavior has changed.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/6357583434257-Why-did-the-coverage-on-my-Frequently-Bought-Together-Recommend-model-decrease

sources.text:
I have set the maxRecommendations to 4, but I'm still only receiving 3 recommendations in the front end. Why is that?
The maxRecommendations parameter is used when you want less recommendations than the model generates.
By default, the Frequently Bought Together (FBT) model recommends up to 3 products, so using a maxRecommendations bigger than 3 won’t have an effect.
You can change the number of recommendations from 3 to 30, and after the model is re-trained, you will see up to 4 recommendations (if the maxRecommendations parameter is set to 4)
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16990894904209-I-have-set-the-maxRecommendations-to-4-but-I-m-still-only-receiving-3-recommendations-in-the-front-end-Why-is-that

sources.text:
Why is my CSV upload to Recommend failing to train the model correctly?
The CSV file upload is intended to be a temporary solution until you set up your events ingestion. We do however recommend using CSVs with samples of real events for optimal training and performance of the models.If your CSV has successfully uploaded but the model does not appear to be trained correctly, here are a few possible reasons:ObjectIDs:The objectIDs included in your CSV file must exist in the index the model is training on.Timestamps:The timestamps should cover a period of at least 30 days. For example, if you have dates starting from 19.01.25, the dates should cover a period up to 18.02.25.The timestamp of each event should be in a standard format: ISO8601 or RFC3339 (with or without the time).Events older than 90 days are ignored.User Tokens:It's important to consider the distribution of userTokens as these will have an impact on Frequently Bought Together and Related Items model training.Since these models mostly look at user behaviour and interaction patterns to make recommendations, there is a likelihood that they might not learn anything if every event has a different user token. Event Types:The eventType field can only take one of 2 specific values: "click" or "conversion". Recommend models do not take into account eventSubType.More information on the format of your CSV file can be found here.Useful Guides:
Uploading events with multiple ObjectIDs via CSV for Recommend models
[Recommend] Why do I get an error when uploading my events from a CSV file?
Can I upload more than 100MB in a CSV of historical events for Recommend?
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/32840230886801-Why-is-my-CSV-upload-to-Recommend-failing-to-train-the-model-correctly

sources.text:
Why is my Recommend Rule not pinning items as expected?
Recommend Rules do not trigger when no recommendations exist - they apply only when the model returns results. This means that while you can pin any item with a Recommend Rule, it will only be pinned if there are recommendations returned on the source item. If no recommendations exist, the rule will not apply.To ensure something is displayed, you can pass fallbackParameters in your Recommend request. Fallback parameters are supported by the following models: Frequently Bought Together, Related Items, Related Content, Trending Items and Looking Similar.Note that rules will not trigger if the recommendations are fallback items.Related Article: Why is my Recommend Rule not boosting items as expected?
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/41486828868369-Why-is-my-Recommend-Rule-not-pinning-items-as-expected

answer.answer:
Algolia Recommend is our recommendations product: you **train machine-learning recommendation models** (typically from click and conversion events) and then **use those models to show recommended items** in your frontend (for example in Magento, the flow is: collect events → train models → enable them in the admin UI) [https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/6262986311441-How-can-I-implement-Recommend-on-my-Magento-store](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/6262986311441-How-can-I-implement-Recommend-on-my-Magento-store).

## What the “Frequently Bought Together” (FBT) model is
FBT is a Recommend model that **uses users’ conversion patterns** to recommend items that were **bought together** (or that “might go well together”) [https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations).

### Model variants (Relaxed vs Strict)
FBT has two settings you can choose from [https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations):

- **Relaxed variant (default)**: recommends items that are *likely* bought together and **uses collaborative filtering to infer relationships**. Example given: if users often buy A with B, and B with C, the model may recommend **C for A** even if A and C weren’t bought together.
- **Strict variant**: only recommends items that were **converted together in the past** (no inferred pairings), so it **typically returns fewer recommendations**.

### What events it learns from (and why relevance can vary)
Recommendation quality depends on the **conversion events used to train the model** [https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations).
If you train using **addToCart conversion events**, that can introduce “noise” (for example, items added to cart but not purchased). The article notes you can use **event customization** to train using only **purchase events** to make results more relevant.

### How many items it returns
- By default, FBT recommends **up to 3 products**, so setting `maxRecommendations` higher than 3 won’t change anything until you change the model’s own maximum [https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16990894904209-I-have-set-the-maxRecommendations-to-4-but-I-m-still-only-receiving-3-recommendations-in-the-front-end-Why-is-that](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16990894904209-I-have-set-the-maxRecommendations-to-4-but-I-m-still-only-receiving-3-recommendations-in-the-front-end-Why-is-that).
- You can change the model’s number of recommendations from **3 to 30** (then after the model is retrained, you can receive more—subject to `maxRecommendations`) [https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16990894904209-I-have-set-the-maxRecommendations-to-4-but-I-m-still-only-receiving-3-recommendations-in-the-front-end-Why-is-that](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16990894904209-I-have-set-the-maxRecommendations-to-4-but-I-m-still-only-receiving-3-recommendations-in-the-front-end-Why-is-that).

### No “fallbackParameters” for FBT
You **can’t use `fallbackParameters` with Frequently Bought Together**—it’s “impossible to emulate based on search parameters alone.” If there isn’t enough data for FBT, it’s better to show a different recommendation type (the article gives “related products” as an example) [https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11036775611537-Why-are-there-no-fallbackParameters-in-the-Frequently-Bought-Together-model](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11036775611537-Why-are-there-no-fallbackParameters-in-the-Frequently-Bought-Together-model).

What are you trying to power with FBT—PDP cross-sells (“customers also bought”), cart upsell, or something else?

I have set the maxRecommendations to 4, but I'm still only receiving 3 recommendations in the front end. Why is that?
The maxRecommendations parameter is used when you want less recommendations than the model generates.
By default, the Frequently Bought Together (FBT) model recommends up to 3 products, so using a maxRecommendations bigger than 3 won’t have an effect.
You can change the number of recommendations from 3 to 30, and after the model is re-trained, you will see up to 4 recommendations (if the maxRecommendations parameter is set to 4)

sources.text:
Why did the coverage on my Frequently Bought Together Recommend model decrease?
Frequently Bought Together does not maximize for coverage, it maximizes for quality recommendations. In this case, it is likely due to the fact that the events have changed for products, meaning customer behavior has changed.

sources.text:
Can I get recommendations for multiple product IDs at once?
If you are using InstantSearch's Recommend widgets (or the deprecated recommend-js UI library), you can pass multiple objectIDs to the objectIDs parameter. When you do this, Algolia returns a single set of aggregated results, ordered by their score.At the moment, this behavior is only supported for the following Recommend models: 
Frequently Bought Together
Related Products 

Each objectID included in the array counts toward the
  number
  of
  requests in your pricing plan.
If you are using the Recommend API get recommendations method to retrieve recommendations, the objectID parameter doesn’t accept an array in the same way as the Recommend widgets. To retrieve recommendations for multiple product IDs, a suggested workaround is:
Send multiple requests.
Include a single objectID per request.
Aggregate the responses on your side.
Display the merged results as a single recommendation set in your frontend.
The apply this workaround to display one set of recommendations on your front end, you can add the helper function's logic used by the Recommend widgets to your own implementation.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/39834047454609-Can-I-get-recommendations-for-multiple-product-IDs-at-once

sources.text:
Why am I only see 3 recommendations for Frequently Bought Together model?
By default, Algolia Recommend shows up to 3 recommendations for Frequently Bought Together.
 
To change this behaviour you can adjust the number by using the maxRecommendations
attribute.relatedProducts({  // ...  maxRecommendations: 5,});



You can also set the maximum number of recommendations per item to 30 in Recommend settings under step 3. Machine learning model.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/16288892997137-Why-am-I-only-see-3-recommendations-for-Frequently-Bought-Together-model

sources.text:
Why am I seeing irrelevant products in Frequently Bought Together recommendations?
The Frequently Bought Together model looks at users' conversion patterns to recommend items that were either bought together or that might go well together. Model VariantsIt worth noting that our FBT model has two settings which you can choose between:Relaxed variant (default)The relaxed variant recommends items that are likely bought together. It uses collaborative filtering to infer relationships from past purchases. For example:
If users often buy product A with product B, and product B with product C
The model can suggest product C for product A, even if those two items weren’t bought together. 
This approach helps increase catalogue discovery, but can introduce inferred (less direct) pairings.Strict variantThe strict variant only recommends items that were converted together in the past. It doesn’t infer new pairings, so it typically returns fewer recommendations.Impact of EventsThe quality of recommendations depends on the conversion events used to train the model.If you are sending an addToCart conversion events, this can introduce some noise. For example, if a user adds an item to cart but does not complete the purchase.To reduce this "noise", (i.e. only shows products that were purchased together as opposed to added to cart together), you can use the event customization feature to train the model using only purchase events. This should result in the model being more relevant.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8616471516945-Why-am-I-seeing-irrelevant-products-in-Frequently-Bought-Together-recommendations

sources.text:
Why are there no fallbackParameters in the Frequently Bought Together model?
You can’t usefallbackParameterswithFrequently Bought Togetheras it’s impossible to emulate based on search parameters alone. If there isn’t enough data forFrequently Bought Together, it’s better to show a different type of recommendation, for example, related products.
The Frequently Bought Together model only makes sense for items that are actually bought together. That’s why there are no fallback recommendations.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/11036775611537-Why-are-there-no-fallbackParameters-in-the-Frequently-Bought-Together-model

sources.text:
Why is my CSV upload to Recommend failing to train the model correctly?
The CSV file upload is intended to be a temporary solution until you set up your events ingestion. We do however recommend using CSVs with samples of real events for optimal training and performance of the models.If your CSV has successfully uploaded but the model does not appear to be trained correctly, here are a few possible reasons:ObjectIDs:The objectIDs included in your CSV file must exist in the index the model is training on.Timestamps:The timestamps should cover a period of at least 30 days. For example, if you have dates starting from 19.01.25, the dates should cover a period up to 18.02.25.The timestamp of each event should be in a standard format: ISO8601 or RFC3339 (with or without the time).Events older than 90 days are ignored.User Tokens:It's important to consider the distribution of userTokens as these will have an impact on Frequently Bought Together and Related Items model training.Since these models mostly look at user behaviour and interaction patterns to make recommendations, there is a likelihood that they might not learn anything if every event has a different user token. Event Types:The eventType field can only take one of 2 specific values: "click" or "conversion". Recommend models do not take into account eventSubType.More information on the format of your CSV file can be found here.Useful Guides:
Uploading events with multiple ObjectIDs via CSV for Recommend models
[Recommend] Why do I get an error when uploading my events from a CSV file?
Can I upload more than 100MB in a CSV of historical events for Recommend?
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/32840230886801-Why-is-my-CSV-upload-to-Recommend-failing-to-train-the-model-correctly

sources.text:
How can I implement Recommend on my Magento store?
Implementing Recommend on your Magento store is a 3 step process:

Collect conversion and click events ( you can skip this step for the Looking Similar Model)
Train Recommend models
Enable the models in Magento Admin to implement them on your front-end

1. Collect conversion and click events
To generate Algolia's recommendations, click events need to be sent from the frontend to Algolia. This guide here explains how to enable those events via your Magento Admin Panel. The events included in the Personalization configuration of the Magento extension can also be used for Recommend.
2. Train Recommend models
Once you have enough events, you can start training the Recommend models as described here. These are the available models you can train.

Frequently Bought Together
Related Products
Trending Items and Trending facet values

Looking Similar (This model can be set up in a few minutes as it doesn’t require any events.)

3. Implement the Recommend UI on your front-end

Once your Recommend models are successfully trained, navigate to Stores > Configuration > Algolia Search > Recommend Products Settings via your Magento Admin Panel to implement the Recommend UI on your front-end. You can refer to the detailed guide here for more details.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/6262986311441-How-can-I-implement-Recommend-on-my-Magento-store

sources.text:
Why did recommend stop working on my website?
 
If a website using Algolia Recommend stopped populating Recommended products after August 2024, it could be related to the major release of the Recommend JavaScript API client v5.
https://github.com/algolia/algoliasearch-client-javascript/tree/main/packages/recommend 
 
This version is a major release from v4 to v5 and is incompatible with the previous version.
That said, when the website loads this library without specifying a version like the following, it loads the latest library, which can cause an issue on the frontend.
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@algolia/recommend"></script>
To prevent the issue, we recommend explicitly specifying the Recommend API client version on the frontend. 
For example, you can use v4.24.0 by adding a script tag like the following.
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@algolia/recommend@4.24.0"></script>
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27757176794513-Why-did-recommend-stop-working-on-my-website

sources.text:
How can I use Algolia Recommend to create a "Complete the Look" solution?
You may be looking to create a 'Complete the Look' experience that suggests complementary products from different categories when a customer views a product detail page? There is no specific 'Complete the Look" model available for Recommend, but here are a couple of existing tools you can use to achieve this with the models that are available:

Content-based filtering for Related Items on Recommendto achieve this, you will need to have a shared attribute indexed on all your products (i.e. completeTheLook:1234_4567) and make sure that this attribute holds the same value for the products you want to present together.


Looking Similar on Recommend
where the model will group together products that are related to each other based on the images provided in your index.
you can choose to index a specific image attribute per record to help the model group together items for the same "look"


Retrieve the Complete-the-look products from a second query
in this case you will need to have a shared attribute like completeTheLook as described above and declared as an attribute for faceting.
You can now send a new second query with a filter (i.e filters:'completeTheLook:value') where the value would be determined by the viewed product details, to retrieve the list of complimentary products.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/36803071711505-How-can-I-use-Algolia-Recommend-to-create-a-Complete-the-Look-solution

sources.text:
[Recommend] Why are my products still pinned when they go out of stock, even though I have a filter to prevent out of stock items being suggested?
It's not possible to use a filter to hide pinned items within Recommend Rules as you would in a general rule. This is because the filterPromotes parameter https://www.algolia.com/doc/api-reference/api-parameters/filterPromotes/#how-to-use , which controls whether promoted results match the filters of the current search, is not available for use within Recommend Rules.
